function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Calculate AI and Connectivity segment capex intensity for FY2025 and Q1 2026. Compare quarter versus full-year intensity and assess what the change implies about the pace and stage of AI infrastructure buildout.

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about AI and Connectivity segment capex intensity for FY2025 and Q1 2026. I need to:

1. First, parse the SpaceX S-1 filing to get the relevant financial data
2. Look for segment-level capital expenditure data and revenue data for the AI and Connectivity segment
3. Calculate capex intensity (capex as a percentage of revenue) for both periods
4. Compare and analyze

Let me start by parsing the filing.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about segment capital expenditure data and revenue for AI and Connectivity segment.

arguments.prompt: What are the capital expenditures or capital spending amounts for the AI and Connectivity segment in FY2025 and Q1 2026 in {{spacex_s1}}? Look for segment-level capital expenditure data, capex by segment, or any capital spending broken down by business segment.

What are the revenue figures for the AI and Connectivity segment for FY2025 and Q1 2026 in {{spacex_s1}}? Look for segment revenue breakdowns, revenue by segment, or any financial data showing revenue for the AI and Connectivity segment.

results.content:

*   **For the year ended December 31, 2025 (FY2025):**
    *   **AI Segment:** $12,727 million
    *   **Connectivity Segment:** $4,178 million

*   **For the three months ended March 31, 2026 (Q1 2026):**
    *   **AI Segment:** $7,723 million
    *   **Connectivity Segment:** $1,332 million

*Source: Preamble section, "Capital expenditures by segment" table.*

Based on the provided documents, here are the revenue figures for the AI and Connectivity segments for **FY2025** (the year ended December 31, 2025) and **Q1 2026** (the three months ended March 31, 2026).

### **FY2025 (Year Ended December 31, 2025)**
*   **Connectivity Segment:** **$11,387 million**
*   **AI Segment:** **$3,201 million**

### **Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026)**
*   **Connectivity Segment:** **$3,257 million**
*   **AI Segment:** **$818 million**

These figures are consistently reported across multiple tables in the documents, including the segment financial results and the disaggregated revenue summaries.

steps.message: Now let me calculate the capex intensity metrics precisely.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the data I need. Let me calculate the capex intensity for both segments.

**AI Segment:**
- FY2025 Capex: $12,727M, Revenue: $3,201M → Capex intensity = 12,727 / 3,201 = 397.6%
- Q1 2026 Capex: $7,723M, Revenue: $818M → Capex intensity = 7,723 / 818 = 944.1%

**Connectivity Segment:**
- FY2025 Capex: $4,178M, Revenue: $11,387M → Capex intensity = 4,178 / 11,387 = 36.7%
- Q1 2026 Capex: $1,332M, Revenue: $3,257M → Capex intensity = 1,332 / 3,257 = 40.9%

**Combined AI and Connectivity:**
- FY2025 Capex: $12,727 + $4,178 = $16,905M, Revenue: $3,201 + $11,387 = $14,588M → Capex intensity = 16,905 / 14,588 = 115.9%
- Q1 2026 Capex: $7,723 + $1,332 = $9,055M, Revenue: $818 + $3,257 = $4,075M → Capex intensity = 9,055 / 4,075 = 222.2%

Let me also annualize Q1 2026 to see if the run-rate implies acceleration.

Annualized Q1 2026:
- AI Capex annualized: $7,723 × 4 = $30,892M
- AI Revenue annualized: $818 × 4 = $3,272M
- AI intensity annualized: 30,892 / 3,272 = 944.1% (same as quarterly since we're comparing ratios)

Actually, for comparison purposes:
- Q1 2026 AI capex = $7,723M vs FY2025 AI capex = $12,727M. Q1 is already 60.7% of the full year 2025.
- Q1 2026 Connectivity capex = $1,332M vs FY2025 Connectivity capex = $4,178M. Q1 is already 31.9% of the full year 2025.

This suggests a massive acceleration in AI capex in Q1 2026.

Let me do the calculations more precisely.
Now let me also calculate the combined "AI and Connectivity" segment capex intensity, and the Q1 as a percentage of FY2025.
Now let me also get the annualized Q1 2026 run-rate comparison. Q1 2026 AI capex annualized = $7,723 × 4 = $30,892M. This would imply a 2.4x increase over FY2025's $12,727M.
Let me also retrieve any discussion in the filing about AI infrastructure buildout, spending plans, or capex guidance to provide more context for the analysis.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about capital expenditure plans, AI infrastructure buildout, spending expectations, or investment plans for 2026 and beyond in {{spacex_s1}}? Look for any forward-looking statements about capex, data center construction, GPU purchases, or infrastructure investment.
Retrieve the full capital expenditures by segment table from {{spacex_s1}} including all segments and both periods (FY2025 and Q1 2026).

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the filing, here are the key forward-looking statements and disclosures regarding capital expenditure, infrastructure investment, and future plans for 2026 and beyond.

### **1. AI Infrastructure & Data Center Investment**
*   **Substantial and Increasing CapEx:** The company states it "expect[s] to increase our capital expenditures substantially in the future" and has "made and intend to continue to make substantial capital expenditures to support the growth of our AI products." This includes costs for third-party GPUs, manufacturing its own GPUs, and constructing/leasing/expanding data centers.
*   **Multi-Year Investment Horizon:** "We expect a multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA." Capital expenditures for AI are expected to "scale as quickly as we are able to deploy power and compute."
*   **Recent and Future Commitments:**
    *   A five-year lease for AI infrastructure hardware valued at **$6.587 billion** (April 2026).
    *   A cloud services agreement for compute capacity with Anthropic through May 2029 (May 2026).
    *   An asset purchase agreement for mobile gas turbines for **~$2 billion** to help power data centers (April 2026, expected to close May 2026).
*   **Construction-in-Progress:** A significant portion of "Property, plant, and equipment" is classified as "Construction-in-progress," which grew from $4.6 billion at year-end 2025 to **$14.045 billion** at the end of Q1 2026.

### **2. Capital Expenditure Trends & Expectations**
*   **Historical Surge:** AI segment capital expenditures exploded from **$463 million** in 2023 to **$12.727 billion** in 2025.
*   **Q1 2026 Acceleration:** In just the first quarter of 2026, AI CapEx was **$7.723 billion**, already representing over 60% of the full year 2025 total, indicating a rapidly accelerating pace.
*   **Funding Strategy:** The company "plan[s] to access a range of debt and equity financing solutions... to fund future investments in growth and to maintain strong liquidity."

### **3. Specific Infrastructure Focus Areas**
*   **Terrestrial Data Centers:** The primary driver of the massive increase in AI CapEx is "investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure."
*   **GPU & Compute Hardware:** Investments include "obtaining third-party GPUs, manufacturing our own GPUs" and building "AI compute infrastructure."
*   **Power & Energy:** The turbine acquisition is specifically to "help provide power to the Company's data centers."

### **4. Long-Term Vision: Orbital AI & Starship**
*   **Starship as an Enabler:** Starship development is framed as a cost-reducer that will "establish a scalable path to creating the infrastructure of the future, such as **orbital AI compute**."
*   **Goal of Dramatic Cost Reduction:** The filing expects Starship to "reduce the cost to reach orbit by 99% or more relative to the historical average launch cost."

### **5. Cautionary Statements & Risks**
*   **Profitability Warning:** The company explicitly warns that it "may choose to increase or accelerate the pace of any of these investments at any time, which could result in periods of **reduced profitability or increased losses** as we prioritize long-term growth over near-term financial performance."
*   **Uncertain Returns:** There "can be no assurance that the returns on our AI investments will be adequate to justify the capital deployed."
*   **Nascent Market Risk:** The "commercial value proposition of frontier AI models remains largely unproven," and the AI segment "has incurred significant operating losses since inception."

**In summary, the filing presents a picture of an aggressive, multi-year investment cycle focused on terrestrial AI data centers and compute hardware, funded through significant financing, with the explicit goal of capturing a large market opportunity while accepting a near-term drag on profitability. The long-term vision ties this to cost-effective space launch capability (Starship) for future orbital infrastructure.**

Let me also compute a few more comparison metrics.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me compile the analysis.

**Summary Data:**

| Metric | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Annualized Q1 2026 |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI Capex | $12,727M | $7,723M | $30,892M |
| AI Revenue | $3,201M | $818M | $3,272M |
| AI Capex Intensity | 397.6% | 944.1% | 944.1% |
| Connectivity Capex | $4,178M | $1,332M | $5,328M |
| Connectivity Revenue | $11,387M | $3,257M | $13,028M |
| Connectivity Capex Intensity | 36.7% | 40.9% | 40.9% |
| Combined Capex | $16,905M | $9,055M | $36,220M |
| Combined Revenue | $14,588M | $4,075M | $16,300M |
| Combined Capex Intensity | 115.9% | 222.2% | 222.2% |

Key observations:
1. Q1 2026 AI capex ($7,723M) already represents 60.7% of full-year 2025 AI capex
2. Q1 2026 AI capex is 3.0x Q1 2025 AI capex ($2,567M)
3. AI capex intensity nearly tripled from ~398% to ~944%
4. Meanwhile AI revenue grew only 12.5% YoY in Q1
5. The annualized Q1 2026 AI capex would imply ~$30.9B for the full year, 2.4x FY2025
6. Connectivity capex intensity is relatively stable at ~37-41%, reflecting a more mature business
7. Construction-in-progress nearly tripled from $4.6B to $14.0B in one quarter

This all points to a massive acceleration in AI infrastructure buildout, with the company clearly in the "heavy investment" phase. The filing explicitly warns of "multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."

arguments.final_result:
## AI and Connectivity Segment Capex Intensity Analysis

### Key Data (from SpaceX S-1, filed May 20, 2026)

**Capital Expenditures by Segment:**

| Segment | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Q1 2026 as % of FY2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| AI | $12,727M | $7,723M | 60.7% |
| Connectivity | $4,178M | $1,332M | 31.9% |
| **Combined** | **$16,905M** | **$9,055M** | **53.6%** |

**Segment Revenue:**

| Segment | FY2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---:|---:|
| AI | $3,201M | $818M |
| Connectivity | $11,387M | $3,257M |
| **Combined** | **$14,588M** | **$4,075M** |

---

### Capex Intensity (Capex ÷ Revenue)

| Segment | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Change |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **AI** | **397.6%** | **944.1%** | **+546.5 pp** |
| **Connectivity** | **36.7%** | **40.9%** | **+4.2 pp** |
| **Combined** | **115.9%** | **222.2%** | **+106.3 pp** |

---

### Annualized Q1 2026 Run-Rate Comparison

If Q1 2026 spending persisted at the same pace for four quarters:

| Segment | Annualized Q1 2026 Capex | vs. FY2025 Capex | Implied Growth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| AI | $30,892M | 2.43× | +143% |
| Connectivity | $5,328M | 1.28× | +28% |
| Combined | $36,220M | 2.14× | +114% |

---

### Year-over-Year Q1 Comparison

| Metric | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 | YoY Growth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| AI Capex | $2,567M | $7,723M | +201% (3.0×) |
| AI Revenue | $727M | $818M | +12.5% |
| AI Capex Intensity | 353.1% | 944.1% | +591.0 pp |

---

### Assessment: What the Change Implies About Pace and Stage of AI Infrastructure Buildout

**1. Q1 2026 represents a dramatic step-change in the pace of AI investment.** AI capex of $7.7B in a single quarter represents 60.7% of the entire FY2025 spend and is 3× the Q1 2025 level. At the annualized run-rate (~$30.9B), the company would deploy nearly 2.4× its full-year 2025 AI capital expenditure. This is not incremental growth — it signals a deliberate acceleration into peak buildout phase.

**2. Capex intensity at ~944% confirms the company is deep in the pre-revenue scaling phase of AI infrastructure.** For every $1 of AI revenue in Q1 2026, SpaceX invested ~$9.44 of capital. This ratio has nearly tripled from ~353% in Q1 2025 (and ~398% for FY2025), indicating that investment is outpacing revenue generation by an increasingly wide margin. The filing explicitly warns of a "multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."

**3. The divergence between capex growth (+201% YoY) and revenue growth (+12.5% YoY) is analytically significant.** AI revenue grew only modestly from $727M to $818M in Q1, while capex tripled. This confirms that the company is building capacity well ahead of current monetization — consistent with frontier AI infrastructure where large fixed investments in data centers, GPUs, and power must precede revenue realization.

**4. Connectivity capex intensity tells a contrasting story of maturity.** At ~37–41%, Connectivity exhibits a capital-light profile typical of an established, cash-generating segment. The modest increase from 36.7% to 40.9% reflects continued but disciplined network expansion (likely Starlink), not a disruptive investment phase. Q1 2026 Connectivity capex of $1,332M represents only 31.9% of FY2025 — a typical seasonal pattern for a mature infrastructure business.

**5. The physical scale-up is corroborated by balance sheet evidence.** Construction-in-progress surged from $4.6B at year-end 2025 to $14.0B at Q1 2026 — a nearly $9.4B increase in a single quarter — reflecting massive ongoing terrestrial data center construction. Post-quarter commitments include a $6.6B five-year hardware lease (April 2026) and a ~$2B mobile gas turbine acquisition to power data centers (April 2026).

**6. Implications for capital intensity trajectory:** The filing states the company "may choose to increase or accelerate the pace of any of these investments at any time." Given that Q1 2026 already represents more than half of FY2025's total AI capex, full-year 2026 AI capex could plausibly exceed $25–30B, potentially pushing combined capex to $40B+ (including Space and Connectivity). This positions SpaceX's AI buildout among the most capital-intensive infrastructure programs in the technology sector, comparable in scale to hyperscaler data center investments but with a substantially smaller current revenue base — resulting in uniquely elevated capex intensity ratios.

**Bottom line:** The transition from ~398% capex intensity (FY2025) to ~944% (Q1 2026) signals SpaceX has moved from an initial investment ramp into an aggressive, peak-capex buildout phase for AI infrastructure. The company is deploying capital at a rate roughly 9× its AI revenue, consistent with constructing foundational compute capacity ahead of commercial monetization. This is characteristic of early-to-mid stage infrastructure buildout where the economic returns will only become visible with a multi-year lag.

results.content:
## AI and Connectivity Segment Capex Intensity Analysis

### Key Data (from SpaceX S-1, filed May 20, 2026)

**Capital Expenditures by Segment:**

| Segment | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Q1 2026 as % of FY2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| AI | $12,727M | $7,723M | 60.7% |
| Connectivity | $4,178M | $1,332M | 31.9% |
| **Combined** | **$16,905M** | **$9,055M** | **53.6%** |

**Segment Revenue:**

| Segment | FY2025 | Q1 2026 |
|---|---:|---:|
| AI | $3,201M | $818M |
| Connectivity | $11,387M | $3,257M |
| **Combined** | **$14,588M** | **$4,075M** |

---

### Capex Intensity (Capex ÷ Revenue)

| Segment | FY2025 | Q1 2026 | Change |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **AI** | **397.6%** | **944.1%** | **+546.5 pp** |
| **Connectivity** | **36.7%** | **40.9%** | **+4.2 pp** |
| **Combined** | **115.9%** | **222.2%** | **+106.3 pp** |

---

### Annualized Q1 2026 Run-Rate Comparison

If Q1 2026 spending persisted at the same pace for four quarters:

| Segment | Annualized Q1 2026 Capex | vs. FY2025 Capex | Implied Growth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| AI | $30,892M | 2.43× | +143% |
| Connectivity | $5,328M | 1.28× | +28% |
| Combined | $36,220M | 2.14× | +114% |

---

### Year-over-Year Q1 Comparison

| Metric | Q1 2025 | Q1 2026 | YoY Growth |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| AI Capex | $2,567M | $7,723M | +201% (3.0×) |
| AI Revenue | $727M | $818M | +12.5% |
| AI Capex Intensity | 353.1% | 944.1% | +591.0 pp |

---

### Assessment: What the Change Implies About Pace and Stage of AI Infrastructure Buildout

**1. Q1 2026 represents a dramatic step-change in the pace of AI investment.** AI capex of $7.7B in a single quarter represents 60.7% of the entire FY2025 spend and is 3× the Q1 2025 level. At the annualized run-rate (~$30.9B), the company would deploy nearly 2.4× its full-year 2025 AI capital expenditure. This is not incremental growth — it signals a deliberate acceleration into peak buildout phase.

**2. Capex intensity at ~944% confirms the company is deep in the pre-revenue scaling phase of AI infrastructure.** For every $1 of AI revenue in Q1 2026, SpaceX invested ~$9.44 of capital. This ratio has nearly tripled from ~353% in Q1 2025 (and ~398% for FY2025), indicating that investment is outpacing revenue generation by an increasingly wide margin. The filing explicitly warns of a "multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA."

**3. The divergence between capex growth (+201% YoY) and revenue growth (+12.5% YoY) is analytically significant.** AI revenue grew only modestly from $727M to $818M in Q1, while capex tripled. This confirms that the company is building capacity well ahead of current monetization — consistent with frontier AI infrastructure where large fixed investments in data centers, GPUs, and power must precede revenue realization.

**4. Connectivity capex intensity tells a contrasting story of maturity.** At ~37–41%, Connectivity exhibits a capital-light profile typical of an established, cash-generating segment. The modest increase from 36.7% to 40.9% reflects continued but disciplined network expansion (likely Starlink), not a disruptive investment phase. Q1 2026 Connectivity capex of $1,332M represents only 31.9% of FY2025 — a typical seasonal pattern for a mature infrastructure business.

**5. The physical scale-up is corroborated by balance sheet evidence.** Construction-in-progress surged from $4.6B at year-end 2025 to $14.0B at Q1 2026 — a nearly $9.4B increase in a single quarter — reflecting massive ongoing terrestrial data center construction. Post-quarter commitments include a $6.6B five-year hardware lease (April 2026) and a ~$2B mobile gas turbine acquisition to power data centers (April 2026).

**6. Implications for capital intensity trajectory:** The filing states the company "may choose to increase or accelerate the pace of any of these investments at any time." Given that Q1 2026 already represents more than half of FY2025's total AI capex, full-year 2026 AI capex could plausibly exceed $25–30B, potentially pushing combined capex to $40B+ (including Space and Connectivity). This positions SpaceX's AI buildout among the most capital-intensive infrastructure programs in the technology sector, comparable in scale to hyperscaler data center investments but with a substantially smaller current revenue base — resulting in uniquely elevated capex intensity ratios.

**Bottom line:** The transition from ~398% capex intensity (FY2025) to ~944% (Q1 2026) signals SpaceX has moved from an initial investment ramp into an aggressive, peak-capex buildout phase for AI infrastructure. The company is deploying capital at a rate roughly 9× its AI revenue, consistent with constructing foundational compute capacity ahead of commercial monetization. This is characteristic of early-to-mid stage infrastructure buildout where the economic returns will only become visible with a multi-year lag.
